core_story.summary: This difficulty arises from three primary factors: Global 3D Consistency. However, text-to-image models often exhibit biases that limit their effectiveness for 3D applications, such as fa- voring frontal views and lacking a robust understanding of 3D structures. Our key insight is to incorporate 3D garment structure information as guidance within a pre-trained 2D image gen- eration model [8], leveraging its generalization capabilities to produce consistent and diverse UV texture maps directly in the 2D UV space through only one inference process. Our method efficiently generates high-quality, diverse UV tex- tures in a single inference step while maintaining 3D con- sistency. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of TexGarment, achieving state-of-the-art performance in 3D garment texture generation.

lights the need for efficient 3D garment generation methods.
The aesthetic appeal of 3D garments is deeply influenced
by their texture quality, as Joseph Albers [1] said: “ Texture
is the soul of painting; it gives surface depth and person-
ality”, effectively capturing this concept. In 3D computer
graphics, textures are essential for realism, adding detailed
surface elements that make virtual objects lifelike. For 3D
garments, high-quality textures are particularly important,
capturing fabric nuances, stitching, folds, and shadows that
make virtual clothing look authentic. Without these tex-
tures, even well-modeled garments can appear flat or artifi-
cial, reducing the immersive quality of 3D applications.

and 3D shape generation using GANs [40, 42] and diffusion
models [12, 38, 39], achieving high-quality garment texture

Methods learning from limited 3D data, such as TexOct [23], PointUV [44], and Mesh2Tex [4], generate textures by explicitly integrat- ing 3D shape information, resulting in high 3D consistency.

However, these approaches often lack generalization abil- ity, limiting their effectiveness when generating textures for diverse 3D objects beyond the training set or adapting to a range of text and visual prompts.

Abstract This paper introduces TexGarment, an efficient method for synthesizing high-quality, 3D-consistent garment textures in UV space.
Additionally, a user study shows that users pre- fer textures generated by our method.

By leveraging pre-trained 2D image generation models and incorporating 3D garment structure information, TexGar- ment addresses key challenges in texture generation, such as global 3D consistency and inference speed.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
This paper introduces TexGarment, an efficient method for
synthesizing high-quality, 3D-consistent garment textures
in UV space. Traditional approaches based on 2D-to-3D
mapping often suffer from 3D inconsistency, while methods
learning from limited 3D data lack sufficient texture diver-
sity. These limitations are particularly problematic in gar-
ment texture generation, where high demands exist for both
detail and variety. To address these challenges, TexGar-
ment leverages a pre-trained text-to-image diffusion Trans-
former model with robust generalization capabilities, intro-
ducing structural information to guide the model in gener-
ating 3D-consistent garment textures in a single inference
step. Specifically, We utilize the 2D UV position map to
*Project lead and corresponding author.
guide the layout during the UV texture generation process,
ensuring a coherent texture arrangement and enhancing it
by integrating global 3D structural information from the
mesh surface point cloud. This combined guidance effec-
tively aligns 3D structural integrity with 2D layout. Our
method efficiently generates high-quality, diverse UV tex-
tures in a single inference step while maintaining 3D con-
sistency. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of
TexGarment, achieving state-of-the-art performance in 3D
garment texture generation.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
The demand for 3D garment visualization is growing across
virtual try-ons for e-commerce, video games, 3D films, and
AR/VR environments. These industries need large volumes
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
26566

of high-quality 3D garments to meet consumer expecta-
tions. While 2D fashion images are widely available and
generative AI has simplified 2D image creation, producing
high-quality 3D garment textures remains challenging, of-
ten requiring skilled artists and weeks of work. This high-
lights the need for efficient 3D garment generation methods.
The aesthetic appeal of 3D garments is deeply influenced
by their texture quality, as Joseph Albers [1] said: “ Texture
is the soul of painting; it gives surface depth and person-
ality”, effectively capturing this concept. In 3D computer
graphics, textures are essential for realism, adding detailed
surface elements that make virtual objects lifelike. For 3D
garments, high-quality textures are particularly important,
capturing fabric nuances, stitching, folds, and shadows that
make virtual clothing look authentic. Without these tex-
tures, even well-modeled garments can appear flat or artifi-
cial, reducing the immersive quality of 3D applications.
Despite substantial advancements in 2D image synthesis
and 3D shape generation using GANs [40, 42] and diffusion
models [12, 38, 39], achieving high-quality garment texture
generation remains challenging. This difficulty arises from
three primary factors: Global 3D Consistency. Methods
employing 2D diffusion models, such as TexPainter [46],
TEXTure [37] and Text2Tex [7], have made strides in tex-
ture synthesis by utilizing pre-trained LDM [38], which
support strong generalization and diverse texture genera-
tion. However, text-to-image models often exhibit biases
that limit their effectiveness for 3D applications, such as fa-
voring frontal views and lacking a robust understanding of
3D structures. This frequently results in texture inconsisten-
cies across different viewpoints of the same 3D object, rang-
ing from minor seam misalignments to severe issues, e.g.,
“Janus effect”. Generalization Ability. Methods learning
from limited 3D data, such as TexOct [23], PointUV [44],
and Mesh2Tex [4], generate textures by explicitly integrat-
ing 3D shape information, resulting in high 3D consistency.
However, these approaches often lack generalization abil-
ity, limiting their effectiveness when generating textures for
diverse 3D objects beyond the training set or adapting to
a range of text and visual prompts. Inference Speed. To
achieve full shape coverage, prior methods [2, 37, 44, 45]
rely on iterative generation processes, requiring multiple
steps or stages. This results in prolonged inference times,
often lasting several minutes, and requires significant com-
putational resources, limiting practical use for designers and
content creators.
In this work, we presentTexGarment, a method tailored
for generating high-quality garment textures. Given text
prompts, TexGarment produces consistent, diverse, and vi-
sually striking textures in a single inference pass. A widely
used approach for handling texture maps is UV representa-
tion, which maps 3D textures accurately onto 2D UV im-
ages through UV coordinate transformations. This tech-
nique bridges the gap between 3D texture generation and
2D image methods, motivating our use of pre-trained image
generation models [8] to synthesize diverse textures effec-
tively within the 2D UV space.
Our key insight is to incorporate 3D garment structure
information as guidance within a pre-trained 2D image gen-
eration model [8], leveraging its generalization capabilities
to produce consistent and diverse UV texture maps directly
in the 2D UV space through only one inference process.
Specifically, our method introduces two main components:
1) 3D Structure Information Extraction and 2) 3D Structure
Guidance. First, given a garment mesh, we use rasterization
to obtain a UV position map, where each pixel encodes the
XYZ coordinates of the corresponding point on the mesh
surface. This UV position map provides a 2D projection of
the original 3D point cloud, capturing the basic layout of
the UV texture. However, regions that are adjacent in 3D
space may be separated in the UV map, causing a loss of
global 3D structure information. This makes it challenging
for a 2D image generation model to capture global 3D struc-
tures solely from the 2D UV position map. To address this,
we introduce a 3D structure information extraction module
that injects global structure information, represented by 3D
point clouds, into the 2D UV position map. This process
generates a 3D structure latent, which combines both 3D
structure and 2D layout information, serving as guidance
for the pre-trained image generation model. Second, we im-
plement a 3D structure guidance module, analogous to text
guidance, which injects the 3D structure information into
the pre-trained model via cross-attention. Using a powerful
text-to-image diffusion model [8] as our basic architecture,
our design enables the model to receive guidance from both
text and 3D structure, allowing it to generate high-quality
UV texture maps in a single inference process, achieving
both text fidelity and 3D consistency.
Our contributions are summarized as follows: 1) We pro-
pose TexGarment, a one-stage method for garment texture
generation that produces high-quality, diverse UV texture
maps for untextured garment meshes in a single inference
process and completes the process in 4 seconds. 2) We in-
troduce two novel modules to incorporate 3D garment struc-
ture information into the 2D image generation model, pro-
viding 3D-consistent guidance for garment texture gener-
ation. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of
these modules in achieving 3D consistency. 3) Qualitative
and quantitative evaluations demonstrate that our method
outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in garment texture
generation. Additionally, a user study shows that users pre-
fer textures generated by our method.
raw_sections.method:
3. Method
Given a 3D garment model and a target texture descrip-
tion, TexGarment efficiently synthesizes high-quality, di-
verse, and consistent garment UV textures. TexGarment
is a Transformer-based diffusion model, as shown in Fig-
ure 2. We build on a large-scale pre-trained image gener-
ation model [8], leveraging its robust prior knowledge to
enhance our model’s generalization capability. To ensure
global 3D consistency, we first design a 3D structure in-
formation extraction module. For an untextured garment
mesh, we apply rasterization to create a UV position map
that captures the basic UV texture layout and encodes the
3D coordinates of points on the mesh surface. The structure
information extraction module then embeds global structure
information from the original point cloud into the UV po-
sition map, resulting in a 3D structure latent that combines
global 3D structure information with 2D layout details. This
3D structure latent is then used by a 3D structure guidance
module to guide the texture generation. Through this inno-
vative design, our method generates high-quality and 3D-
consistent texture maps in a single inference process, with-
out the need for additional operations such as inpainting.
The following sections provide the overview of each stage.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusions
In conclusion, we present TexGarment, a novel and effi-
cient method for generating high-quality garment textures.
By leveraging pre-trained 2D image generation models and
incorporating 3D garment structure information, TexGar-
ment addresses key challenges in texture generation, such
as global 3D consistency and inference speed. Our method
enables the generation of diverse and visually consistent UV
texture maps in a single inference process, significantly im-
proving on previous multi-stage approaches. Through the
introduction of two key components: 3D structure informa-
tion extraction and 3D structure guidance, we effectively
integrate 3D structural details into the 2D texture genera-
tion process, resulting in high fidelity and 3D consistency.
Our experiments show that TexGarment outperforms ex-
isting methods in both quality and performance, with user
studies confirming its superior visual appeal.
Limitation. TexGarment now does not support image-
conditioned generation. In practical applications, users may
provide a reference image to generate garment textures in a
similar style. We will explore it in future research.
Acknowledgments. This work was supported in part by
the National Natural Science Foundation of China under
grant 62202369.
